coredata.dc:title: Effects of gel-like carbon dots (G-CDs) and surfactant (Span80) on the droplet combustion dynamics of liquid fuels

coredata.dc:creator: Parveg, A S M Sazzad
Zhou, Yiqun
Leblanc, Roger M.
Ratner, Albert
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the effect of gel-like carbon dots (G-CDs) on the droplet combustion behavior of single-component (Dodecane) and multi-component (Jet-A) fuels using the suspended droplet method. G-CDs were added at concentrations ranging from 0.01% to 1.00% w/w to assess the influence of particle concentration and small G-CD size (∼3.00 nm) on combustion dynamics. A concentration-dependent, non-linear effect was observed on combustion rate and pre-ignition time. At concentrations ≤0.10% w/w, significant improvements in both combustion rate and pre-ignition time were noted for both fuels. In single-component fuel, the highest combustion rate increase (16.46%) occurred at 0.10% w/w, with the greatest reduction in pre-ignition time (10.19%) at 0.075% w/w. For multi-component fuel, the highest increase in combustion rate (16.46%) was also seen at 0.10% w/w, with pre-ignition time reduced by 8.38% at 0.05% w/w. Beyond 0.50% w/w, combustion rate declined due to nanoparticle aggregation, with a 5.00% reduction at 1.00% w/w. G-CDs intensified puffing in multi-component fuels, with puffing intensity and frequency following a linear trend with particle concentration, significantly increasing above 0.10% w/w. In single-component fuel, combustion was primarily influenced by the improved heat absorption, heat transfer, and nanoparticle aggregation. However, in multi-component fuel, puffing played a more significant role. Surfactant effect was also evaluated, with low concentrations (up to 0.50% w/w) stabilizing the fuel without significantly affecting combustion. At higher concentration (2.50% w/w), intense puffing increased the combustion rate by 15.00%. Overall, this study highlights the potential of G-CDs to significantly influence combustion behavior of liquid fuel and offers valuable preliminary insights into their application in combustion systems.

head.ce:title: Effects of gel-like carbon dots (G-CDs) and surfactant (Span80) on the droplet combustion dynamics of liquid fuels

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the effect of gel-like carbon dots (G-CDs) on the droplet combustion behavior of single-component (Dodecane) and multi-component (Jet-A) fuels using the suspended droplet method. G-CDs were added at concentrations ranging from 0.01% to 1.00% w/w to assess the influence of particle concentration and small G-CD size (∼3.00 nm) on combustion dynamics. A concentration-dependent, non-linear effect was observed on combustion rate and pre-ignition time. At concentrations ≤0.10% w/w, significant improvements in both combustion rate and pre-ignition time were noted for both fuels. In single-component fuel, the highest combustion rate increase (16.46%) occurred at 0.10% w/w, with the greatest reduction in pre-ignition time (10.19%) at 0.075% w/w. For multi-component fuel, the highest increase in combustion rate (16.46%) was also seen at 0.10% w/w, with pre-ignition time reduced by 8.38% at 0.05% w/w. Beyond 0.50% w/w, combustion rate declined due to nanoparticle aggregation, with a 5.00% reduction at 1.00% w/w. G-CDs intensified puffing in multi-component fuels, with puffing intensity and frequency following a linear trend with particle concentration, significantly increasing above 0.10% w/w. In single-component fuel, combustion was primarily influenced by the improved heat absorption, heat transfer, and nanoparticle aggregation. However, in multi-component fuel, puffing played a more significant role. Surfactant effect was also evaluated, with low concentrations (up to 0.50% w/w) stabilizing the fuel without significantly affecting combustion. At higher concentration (2.50% w/w), intense puffing increased the combustion rate by 15.00%. Overall, this study highlights the potential of G-CDs to significantly influence combustion behavior of liquid fuel and offers valuable preliminary insights into their application in combustion systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofuels, which consist of liquid fuel mixed with nanoparticles (NPs), are being researched for their potential to enhance internal combustion engine performance and reduce emissions. These fuels improve combustion efficiency, increase power output, and alter emission profiles by affecting the combustion rate, energy density, ignition delay, catalytic activity and heat release rate [1–12]. Typically containing less than 1.00% NPs by weight, nanofuels were tested to optimize key metrics such as thermal efficiency, specific fuel consumption, and emissions of pollutants like NOx, CO, CO2, unburned hydrocarbons (UHC), and soot [2,3,6–10]. Enhanced thermal conductivity and improved thermal radiation absorption in nanofuels significantly boost heat absorption and transfer within fuel droplets. This accelerates the evaporation rate, leading to higher combustion efficiency and improved overall engine performance [1–3,6–10]. The catalytic activity of NPs speeds up chemical reactions by reducing induction times and activation energies, with smaller NPs being more effective in the oxidation of CO and hydrocarbons or the reduction of NOx [2,6–10]. Additionally, energetic NPs can increase the total heat release, while metallic NPs may serve as catalysts to enhance soot oxidation [2]. NPs also influence fuel atomization, causing phenomena like puffing and microexplosions, which improve fuel evaporation and droplet formation dynamics—key factors for increasing combustion efficiency and controlling emissions [2,3].

Understanding the evaporation and combustion dynamics of nanofuel droplets is important for optimizing internal combustion engines. Experimental studies typically focus on isolated droplet combustion to examine the effects of factors like droplet size, shape, ambient conditions, and nanoparticles (NPs) characteristics. Surfactants, used to stabilize NPs, further complicate these dynamics due to their distinct properties compared to both base fuel and NPs, affecting combustion rate, pre-ignition time, and inducing puffing and microexplosions [2–4,11,12].

Gan and Qiao [5] studied the evaporation of high-volatile (Ethanol) and low-volatile (n-Decane) fuels containing aluminum nanoparticles (Al NPs), observing deviations from the classical
d
2
-law at higher NPs concentrations and lower convection temperatures, particularly in n-Decane due to increase NPs aggregation. Similarly, Li et al. [13] explored n-Decane-based nanofuel droplets with graphite nanoplatelets (GrNPs) and Sorbitan monooleate (Span80), finding that increasing GrNPs concentrations enhanced thermal conductivity and reduced latent heat of vaporization. Evaporation rates followed a non-linear trend, peaking at intermediate concentrations (1.00–2.00% w/w), with higher concentrations leading to dense aggregates near the droplet surface, reducing evaporation rates. Aboalhamayie et al. [14] studied Jet-A fuel with multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWNTs), activated carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), and graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs), observing significant improvements in evaporation rates at optimal NPs concentrations, which were dependent on the nanoparticles' thermo-physical properties. Mei et al. [15] reported temperature-dependent evaporation behaviors in diesel fuel containing MWNTs and CeO2 NPs, where smaller NPs enhanced evaporation by acting as nucleation sites. Additionally, Gan and Qiao [16] found that carbon-based NPs (CNPs, MWNTs) in Ethanol exhibited superior radiation absorption, leading to enhanced evaporation rate. Tanvir and Qiao [17] explored the effects of GrNPs in Ethanol under infrared (IR) radiation, noting an initial increase in evaporation rate due to radiation absorption, followed by a decrease as particles accumulated on the droplet surface, reducing the effective evaporation area.

In a separate study, Gan and Qiao [18] investigated the combustion and aggregation dynamics of aluminum nanoparticles (Al NPs)-based nanofuels using both low-volatile (n-Decane) and high-volatile (Ethanol) base fuels. In nanofuels, perikinetic aggregation, driven by Brownian motion, is the predominant mechanism for particle aggregation, while orthokinetic aggregation, which results from droplet surface regression, plays a supplementary role. Internal circulation and differential settling have minimal influence on the aggregation process. As a result, the rapid diffusion of NPs leads to uniformly distributed, spherical aggregates. Ethanol-based nanofuels exhibited microexplosions before ignition due to the fuel's high volatility, leading to frequent droplet fragmentation and enhancing the combustion of aluminum particles during both classical and microexplosion stages. Tanvir and Qiao [19] further investigated thermal radiation absorption in nanofuels with graphite nanoplatelets (GrNPs) suspended in Ethanol, studying different particle sizes (50 nm, 100 nm) and concentrations (0–3.00% w/w). They found that increasing the particle concentration significantly boosted combustion rates, with a peak increase of 62.00% observed at 3.00% w/w for 50 nm particles. A Monte Carlo model estimated radiation absorptivity, showing that localized absorption near the droplet surface promotes surface boiling and accelerates the combustion process. Absorptivity increased with particle concentration, particularly for smaller particles and shorter wavelengths. At low concentrations, volumetric absorption optimized energy for vaporization, but at higher concentrations, aggregation effects led to a plateau in combustion rate improvements. Ghamari and Ratner [20] evaluated various carbon-based NPs (CNPs, MWNTs, MWNTs-OH, GNPs) in Jet-A fuel, finding that burning rates increased by 7.00–10.00%, depending on particle size and morphology. MWNTs led to over 10.00% improvement at 0.25% w/w. However, aggregation reduced combustion rates beyond optimal concentrations. Similarly, Yadav et al. [21] assessed the impact of specific surface areas and concentrations (0–0.50% w/w) of GNPs in Isrosene fuel. At low concentrations, burning adhered to the
d
2
-law, with weak microexplosions, while at higher concentrations, microexplosions became more severe. The aggregation of GNPs at higher concentrations also influenced burning rate.

The practical use of nanofuels in combustion systems faces several challenges despite their advantages. A key issue is nanoparticles (NPs) aggregation, which affects fuel homogeneity and can alter pre-ignition reactions by changing NPs distribution and physical properties [2,3]. Metal-based NPs, such as aluminum (Al), can enhance reaction rates and increase energy density but often face limitations due to non-reactive oxide layers. Moreover, the combustion of metal NPs can result in unburnt residues or metal oxide particulates, which contribute to harmful exhaust emissions, raising environmental and health concerns [3,22–25]. Addressing these challenges requires developing stable nanofuel suspensions that are cost-effective, accessible, and environmentally friendly. Carbon-based NPs, particularly smaller ones, are promising for enhancing liquid fuel combustion without the toxicity risks associated with metal-based NPs [2,3]. Carbon NPs generally have lower thermal degradation temperatures, leading to more complete combustion and fewer residual byproducts. This makes them ideal for applications demanding cleaner combustion processes. Recent advancements in carbon NPs-based nanofuels have focused on novel carbon nanomaterials, hybrid nanofluids incorporating multiple NPs, and functionalization or doping techniques to improve NPs performance [10,26–28]. These innovations aim to optimize combustion efficiency while managing emissions, aligning with increasingly stringent environmental regulations.

Carbon dots (CDs), with their nanoscale dimensions and biocompatibility, offer substantial potential in energy and thermal management applications, particularly in the fields of green and alternative energy [29–35]. Various types of CDs, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), exhibit unique properties that make them attractive for such applications. CDs are known for their high electron transfer capability, photoluminescence, and ease of functionalization. These properties allow their thermo-physical characteristics to be tailored for specific uses, such as in nanofluids for enhanced thermal management or nanofuels for improved combustion efficiency [29–31].

Studies on carbon dots (CDs)-based nanofluids have shown notable improvements in both thermal conductivity and stability. For instance, Ameri et al. [36] reported enhanced colloidal stability and thermal conductivity in graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/water nanofluids, while Zhou et al. [37] found similar improvements with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in ethylene glycol/water mixtures. Additionally, Sedaghat et al. [38] demonstrated that GQDs-based nanofluids enhanced thermal conductivity across various base fluids. Similarly, Shahsavar et al. [39] observed significant increases in thermal conductivity in CDs/ethylene glycol nanofluids at higher concentrations, and Sadeghalvaad et al. [40] found increased density and viscosity in CDs/water nanofluids, emphasizing their stability under ultrasonication and surfactant use. Ettefaghi et al. [41] integrated GQDs into car radiator coolants, reporting a 5.20% increase in thermal conductivity and a 17.00% improvement in heat transfer coefficients. In another study, Zhou et al. [37] achieved a maximum photothermal conversion efficiency of 71.09% in CQDs-based nanofluids for Direct Absorption Solar Collectors (DASC), while Joseph et al. [42] reported a 73.41% thermal efficiency with CQDs/water nanofluids, surpassing the base fluid by 57.62%.

CDs have also been investigated for other applications. Ross et al. [43] found that incorporating CDs into rice bran oil (RBO) as a cutting fluid significantly enhanced thermal conductivity at a concentration of 1.00% w/w. Sadeghalvaad et al. [40] further improved heater bath performance by reducing power consumption by up to 10.30% using CDs/water nanofluids. In the fuel sector, CDs have been used in diesel and gasoline fuels up to 2.00% (v/v) for fuel adulteration detection without compromising fuel properties [44]. Their catalytic effects and ability to enhance secondary atomization were also found to reduce emissions and improve fuel economy during vehicle tests. In the oil and gas industry, CDs have been shown to reduce injection pressure and enhance oil recovery in low-permeability reservoirs [45]. They have also been employed as green tracers in inter-well applications without affecting the properties of injection fluids or crude oil [46]. Additionally, CDs have been tested in solid rocket fuels, resulting in a 20.00% improvement in thermal conductivity and enhanced combustion efficiency (by 8.50%) in ballistic tests [47]. In solid propellants like TKX-50, CDs act as catalysts, improving ignition characteristics and combustion efficiency by modifying chemical kinetics [48]. In automotive applications, GQDs in diesel–biodiesel-water blends have been shown to increase engine power and reduce fuel consumption, though with slight increases in soot emissions [49]. However, uncertainties remain regarding the individual contributions of GQDs as specific insights into their isolated effects were not reported.

Carbon dots (CDs) are increasingly recognized for their ability to enhance heat transfer properties and are now being explored in various liquid fuel applications. There is growing interest in systematically evaluating their impact on combustion processes, particularly at the droplet scale. Due to their smaller size compared to other nanoparticles used in liquid fuels, CDs are expected to have unique effects on combustion dynamics, especially concerning aggregation behavior [2–5,13–15]. The current study aims to address this research interest by characterizing the droplet combustion properties of liquid fuels infused with CDs, specifically the gel-like variants (G-CDs). G-CDs have shown significant performance improvements in solid rocket fuels, attributed to their ability to reduce viscosity, improve particle dispersion, enhance thermal conductivity, and demonstrate catalytic activity [47] These properties make G-CDs a promising candidate for assessing the effects of CDs on liquid fuel combustion. Additionally, G-CDs are biocompatible, biodegradable, and environmentally friendly, making them suitable for various applications [29,50–52]. Their synthesis process allows for customization, tailoring surface functionalities to meet specific needs. The gel-like nature of G-CDs also enhances operational safety and offers insights into their potential interactions with different fuel matrices.

This research aims to systematically investigate the droplet combustion dynamics of nanofuels containing gel-like carbon dots (G-CDs) and surfactants using two base fuels: single-component n-Dodecane and multicomponent Jet-A (with Dodecane as a surrogate). The study focuses on how varying G-CDs concentrations (0.01–1.00% w/w) influence combustion behavior at the droplet scale, particularly in relation to G-CDs aggregation dynamics. Additionally, it explores how Dodecane and Jet-A interact with G-CDs under controlled conditions. This approach helps isolate the effects of multicomponent fuels and accurately evaluate the role of G-CDs. The influence of surfactants is also evaluated, with attention to their effect on fuel combustion rate. The overall objective is to advance understanding of G-CDs infused nanofuels, aiming to improve fuel efficiency, reduce emissions, and optimize combustion processes for various applications.

The synthesis and characterization of the G-CDs used in this study were previously reported by Zhou et al. [50,51]. G-CDs were prepared by placing 5 mL of 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) in a 50 mL round-bottom flask under argon flux and heating to 160 °C, followed by the addition of 1 g of citric acid. The mixture was stirred vigorously for 50 min. After cooling, the G-CDs formed and were dispersed in deionized water, then subjected to vacuum evaporation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) revealed the atomic composition of G-CDs as approximately 75.10% carbon, 19.00% oxygen, and 5.70% nitrogen. The molecular weight analysis indicated that one citric acid molecule bonds with three EDA molecules, suggesting a simple repeated unit within the structure. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) confirmed that the G-CDs had quasi-spherical geometry with an average diameter of 3.00 nm (Fig. 1
a). The UV–Visible spectra of G-CDs synthesized at different temperatures (120 °C, 140 °C, 160 °C, and 180 °C) revealed important structural insights [50] (Fig. 1 b). The spectra showed a consistent band at 332 nm across all samples, corresponding to the n–π* transition of C=O bonds. A π–π* transition band at 266 nm, attributed to aromatic C=C bonds, was observed for G-CDs synthesized at 120 °C, with hypsochromic and hyperchromic shifts in the C=C and C=O bands with the increase in synthesis temperature. These shifts indicate changes in the degree of conjugation within the G-CDs structure at higher temperatures. Important properties of G-CDs are summarized in Table 1 .

n-Dodecane was chosen as the single-component base fuel, while Jet-A served as the multicomponent base fuel. Dodecane is a common surrogate for jet fuel in combustion research because its physical and chemical properties are similar to those of real jet fuels like Jet-A [53,54]. This similarity allows for the study of combustion characteristics, such as evaporation, ignition, and droplet dynamics, in a controlled and simplified manner. This approach helps isolate the effects of multicomponent fuels and accurately evaluate the role of G-CDs. Sorbitan monooleate (Span80) was employed as a surfactant to uniformly and stably disperse G-CDs within the liquid phase. Important properties of the base fuels and surfactant are provided in Table 2 .

The nanofuel preparations were carefully executed in 20 mL disposable scintillation vials obtained from Fisher Scientific, following a two-step protocol as follows. Using an ultrasonic disruptor (Biologics 300MP) equipped with a 3/16″ probe, the disruptive process continued for 60 min, consisting of 4-second pulses at 30% amplitude. To minimize heat generation, the preparation vial was submerged in a water bath, ensuring stable suspensions without undesired agglomeration. Span80 (Sorbitan Monooleate, C24H44O6) was carefully introduced as a surfactant to enhance stability and prevent coagulation of G-CDs in the base fuel. Span80 is frequently used to disperse materials that are insoluble in alcohol and is commonly employed in cosmetics, food, and pharmaceuticals due to its low hazardous profile [55,56]. Additionally, it has been extensively studied for its effectiveness in dispersing nanoparticles in liquid fuels [2–4,11,20]. All nanofuels and base fuel-Span80 mixtures were assigned unique names following a specific naming convention. For base fuel-Span80 mixtures, the format is “XSY.YYY,” where “X” represents the first letter of the base fuel used (D for dodecane, J for Jet-A), “S” stands for surfactant, and “Y.YYY” denotes the surfactant concentration (e.g., 2.50% w/w is represented by 2.50). For example, a 0.50% w/w surfactant mixture with Jet-A is named “JS0.50.” Nanofuels are named using the format “XCDY.YYY,” where “CD” indicates carbon dots. For instance, dodecane-based nanofuels with a 0.25% w/w particle concentration are named “DCD0.25.” The concentrations of the mixtures and nanofuel components, along with their respective names, are detailed in Table S1 of the supplementary materials.

Nanofuel stability was periodically monitored visually, utilizing the unique photoluminescent properties of G-CDs (Fig. 2 ). Under normal lighting conditions, both pure Jet-A and Dodecane appeared transparent, while nanofuels were observed to have a cloudy appearance due to their colloidal nature. When exposed to UV light from a commercially available LED-based UV flashlight, which emitted light in the 390–395 nm wavelength range, the nanofuels exhibited a distinct bluish hue due to the presence of G-CDs. This visual cue allowed for easy differentiation of the nanofuels from the pure Jet-A and Dodecane. Stability was assessed by monitoring the sedimentation of particles over time, with all nanofuel samples demonstrating stability for at least 48 h. To ensure consistency in the experimental procedures, all combustion tests were conducted within 2 h of nanofuel preparation to minimize the potential effects of any sedimentation on combustion behavior.

In this study, seven different concentrations of G-CDs were investigated, specifically 0.01%, 0.05%, 0.075%, 0.10%, 0.25%, 0.50%, and 1.00% w/w. For each concentration, the amount of Span80 surfactant was set to half the mass of the G-CDs. This ratio was established through preliminary testing to ensure effective nanoparticle dispersion while minimizing the influence of the surfactant on combustion behavior, allowing for the sole contribution of G-CDs to be captured in the combustion behavior of fuel droplets. Two main criteria guided the selection of these concentrations: (1) achieving stable dispersion of nanoparticles in the liquid fuel, and (2) reducing any potential impact of Span80 on combustion characteristics. Stability tests involved varying the combinations of G-CDs and Span80 concentrations, with stability visually assessed based on the absence of sedimentation in the sample vials over time. Additionally, changes in the photoluminescence (e.g., shifts in the bluish hue) of G-CDs were monitored to detect any signs of particle agglomeration or instability. The results showed that a Span80 concentration equal to half the G-CDs mass was sufficient to maintain stability for at least 48 h. Since surfactants like Span80 can have concentration-dependent effects on combustion properties, such as burning rate, pre-ignition time, and puffing behavior of fuel droplets, further experiments were conducted to ensure that the concentrations used in this study did not alter combustion performance [11,12]. Tests were carried out using three different surfactant concentrations: 0.05%, 0.50%, and 2.50% w/w. The 0.50% w/w limit matched the highest concentration used in the nanofuel suspensions, while the 2.50% w/w concentration was included to evaluate potential combustion changes at higher surfactant levels.

This study utilized an established experimental approach involving suspended single droplets positioned at the intersection of three silicon carbide (SiC) micro-fibers. Conducted under standard gravity conditions with typical atmospheric parameters (∼25 °C, 1 atm), the setup comprises several integral components, including a sturdy aluminum base plate supported by optical rods, fiber support, ignition mechanisms, and an image acquisition system, as shown in Fig. 3 . The droplets were supported using a cross-fiber technique with NL-202 Nicalon™ micro-fibers (16 μm diameter). A Hamilton micro-syringe (10 µL, Model 701 RN, 26 s gauge) was used to dispense the liquid drops at the fiber juncture. The ignition of liquid droplets was achieved through a controlled hot wire mechanism using Kanthal A-1 wire, with retraction solenoids employed for precise hot wire placement. This study employed two high-speed cameras for detailed analysis of the droplet combustion process. The primary camera, an IDT X-StreamVision XS-3 CCD camera, operated at 1000 frames per second (fps) with a resolution of 944×552 pixels. Paired with a 105 mm lens (Nikon AF MicroNikkor-F/2.8), it provided precise imaging of the droplet combustion scene. Illumination for shadowgraph imaging was provided by a single bright white LED positioned opposite the CCD camera. Control of the CCD camera was managed through IDT Motion Studio software, enabling programming of capture speeds to match the camera's capabilities. Acquired high speed images were used to accurately measure droplet area regression and temporal changes in droplet dynamics and morphology, facilitating quantitative analysis of the combustion process. A color CMOS camera (Casio EXILIM Pro EX-F1) operating at 600 fps and a resolution of 432×192 pixels recorded the droplet combustion process through a 4″ diameter, 9″ focal length magnifying concave mirror. The 600-fps setting provided sufficient image resolution and a wide field of view to cover important objects while accurately capturing events with a time resolution of 1.67 ms between consecutive frames. This camera focused on capturing visual documentation of flame initiation and extinction phases, allowing for quantification of pre-ignition time, combustion duration, and qualitative assessment of flame dynamics. An Arduino UNO synchronized the solenoids and hot wire loops with the CCD camera, operating on a dedicated power circuit.

Suspended droplet combustion experiments can be influenced by various experimental conditions, which may also introduce experimental artifacts. The most important factors include initial droplet size (
d
0
), support fiber’s thermo-physical properties, initial droplet diameter-to-fiber diameter ratio (
d
0
/
d
fiber
) and instantaneous droplet diameter-to-fiber diameter ratio (
d
s
( t ) /
d
f i b e r
). Previous research indicates that a minimum initial droplet-to-fiber diameter ratio (
d
0
/
d
fiber
) of ≥36 is necessary for reliable combustion rate quantification in normal gravity experiments [59]. Additionally, maintaining an instantaneous droplet-to-fiber diameter ratio (
d
s
( t ) /
d
f i b e r
) of ≥13 is essential during combustion; below this threshold, the support fibers can influence the combustion rate [60]. The initial droplet size should ideally be below 1.00 mm to mitigate effects such as soot formation and radiative heat losses [61]. Using microfibers with low thermal conductivity (∼1.50 W/mK) minimizes heat transfer effects, ensuring that the fibers have minimal influence on the combustion process [62].

For this study, the corresponding values include initial droplet size: d 0=∼0.86±0.047 mm, micro-fiber size:
d
fiber
=0.016 mm, fiber thermal conductivity: λfiber=1.50 W/mk, and the droplet-to-fiber diameter ratio
d
0
/
d
fiber
=50∼59. The combustion rate and quantitative data were quantified within a specified range while adhering to the criterion of (
d
s
( t ) /
d
f i b e r
)>20. Furthermore, temporal changes in droplet shape, measured through roundness (O), were assessed to confirm the preservation of the spherical form of burning droplets, particularly during the analysis period for quantitative data.

The primary tool used for image post-processing was Fiji ImageJ software, where the Trainable Weka Segmentation plugin (version 3.3.1) was incorporated for droplet isolation and segmentation from the background and support fiber [63,64]. Fig. 4 illustrates the steps involved in the image post-processing operation and provides a concise overview of the process. The raw image dataset from each experiment consisted of a minimum of 2,000 images, each captured at a resolution of 944×552 pixels. A Region of Interest (ROI) was defined (209×292 pixels) within each image to focus the analysis on specific areas containing the droplets and other relevant features. Image datasets were extracted based on the selected region of interest for further analysis. The Fast Random Forest algorithm, a supervised machine learning technique, was employed for segmentation to accurately identify droplets, background, and support fiber regions. Specific areas within the images were manually annotated to mark the droplet region, background, and fiber, guiding the segmentation process. Labeled images served as ground truth data, helping the segmentation model accurately identify and segment droplet regions. Segmentation outcomes were visually evaluated against the original droplet boundaries, with probability maps generated to assess the confidence levels associated with the segmentation results. Grayscale images were binarized to simplify further analysis by converting pixel values into binary format (black/white). Morphological operations, such as filling holes, were applied to refine segmentation results and ensure continuous, closed shapes. Calibration was performed using a reference standard (Aluminum 2017 Alloy calibration ball, diameter 1/32″) to accurately scale measurements to real-world units. Important metrics, including droplet area, major and minor axes, roundness, and aspect ratio, were extracted from the segmented images, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of droplet behavior during combustion. Color images from the CMOS camera underwent minimal post-processing, primarily cropping for analysis. These images provided visual insights into flame dynamics. Each image represented a specific time interval, facilitating tracking of ignition initiation and flame extinction. The method chosen to quantify droplet size is crucial for assessing combustion dynamics. Despite minor variations among different diameter definitions for nearly perfect spherical droplets, typically less than 2.00%, their impact on combustion rates is negligible, varying by less than 1.00% [65]. Therefore, to ensure experimental reliability, a droplet size definition based on the equivalent volume of a sphere was adopted. This approach can accommodate deviations from perfect sphericity, such as elliptical shapes, by utilizing formulas based on the droplet's semi-major and semi-minor axis lengths. The definition is provided below:
(1)
d
eqvs
= 2
A
1 3
B
2 3
where
A
and
B
are the semi-major and semi-minor axis lengths, respectively, associated with an ellipsoidal droplet.

The primary uncertainty in this study revolves around accurately quantifying droplet size. The temporal resolutions of the CCD and CMOS cameras were measured at 0.001s and 0.0016s, respectively, while the spatial resolution of the CCD camera was approximately 0.007 mm/pixel. To validate the accuracy of droplet diameter measurements, segmented droplet images obtained from ImageJ were compared against manually calculated diameters from a subset of 500 randomly selected images, which spanned different stages of the combustion process. Segmentation uncertainty remained below 5.00% for (
d
/
d
0
)2>0.15, but increased substantially to up to 30% for (
d
/
d
0
)2<0.15. Consequently, only data with (
d
/
d
0
)2>0.15 were considered for quantitative calculations, such as determining combustion rates, to ensure higher accuracy and reliability. The details on the combustion stage identification, combustion rate, combustion time and pre-ignition time quantification are provided in supplementary material under S1: Approaches for Data Quantification. Each type of fuel underwent at least five repeated experiments in this study to ensure robustness and reliability of the results. The uncertainty among these different experiments was quantified using the standard deviation of the mean and is represented as error bars in the quantitative results.

The droplet combustion behavior of single component base fuel: n-Dodecane, and multi-component base fuel: Jet-A have significant differences. Fig. 5
a and 5b represent the temporal changes in droplet morphology of a Dodecane droplet (initial diameter,
d
0
=0.885 mm) and Jet-A droplet (initial diameter,
d
0
=0.872 mm) during combustion, respectively. In these figures, "
t /
d
0
2
" represents normalized time, "
O
" denotes the roundness of the droplet (indicating how spherical the droplet is and any shape alterations during combustion), "
d
" is the instantaneous droplet diameter, (
d
/
d
0
)2 is the normalized droplet diameter,
d f
is the support fiber size, and the blue dotted line represents the ROI where the combustion rates were quantified. The (
d
/
d
0
)2 vs
t /
d
0
2
plots (termed as the "
d
2
-plot") of these two droplets are compared in Fig. 5 c. Before ignition, each droplet underwent volumetric expansion stage (stage 1 in Fig. 5 c) during the heat up period, where thermal energy from the heating source was absorbed by the droplet, raising its temperature near the boiling point of the respective fuels, and causing evaporation or fuel vapor formation. When this fuel vapor mixed with ambient air in stoichiometric conditions, ignition occurred. Ignition happened at
t /
d
0
2
=0.569 and 0.530 s/mm2 for the Dodecane and Jet-A droplets, respectively. The shorter ignition time for the Jet-A droplet was due to the presence of high-volatile components, allowing sufficient fuel vapor formation earlier to initiate ignition under the same heating energy and ambient conditions. After ignition, the Dodecane droplet underwent a steady combustion stage, with its diameter decreasing linearly with time, following the
d
2
-law until flame extinction (
t /
d
0
2
=2.021 s/mm2). Throughout combustion, the Dodecane droplet remained nearly perfectly spherical (
O
>0.980). Conversely, Jet-A droplet regressed almost linearly with time up to
t /
d
0
2
=1.867 s/mm2 (stage 2: Steady combustion). Beyond this point, the volatility differences between surface and core components became significant, leading to superheating of the high-volatile components near the droplet core (Jet-A boiling point ∼425–555 K). This superheating was possibly due to the combined effect of a higher concentration of less volatile components near the surface, dictating droplet temperature, and additional heat input from the support fiber due to a low droplet-diameter-to-support-fiber-size ratio (
d /
d f
). This condition had been reported previously, where heat input from the support fiber affected the combustion process [15,66]. Superheating of high-volatile components caused vapor bubbles to form and grow, expanding the droplet size and ultimately resulting in sudden bursting of these bubbles, altering the droplet shape (as indicated by "
O
") and forming single or multiple secondary droplets. This phenomenon, known as "Puffing", is common during multicomponent droplet combustion [2–4]. The intensity of puffing could be qualitatively distinguished based on the extent of droplet or bubble expansion, the number of secondary droplets generated, or the extent of droplet deformation during puffing. The Jet-A droplet exhibited puffing only near the end of the combustion process, beyond the region of interest (ROI) used for characterizing the combustion behavior. As a result, puffing did not affect the quantitative measurements. Although Jet-A is a multicomponent fuel, puffing was observed only toward the final stages of combustion, having a minimal impact on the overall combustion dynamics.

Fig. 5 d shows the average
d
2
-plots of Dodecane and Jet-A droplets with five experimental repetitions, demonstrating almost similar regression trends despite different regression rates. Fig. 5 e displays three combustion parameters: normalized pre-ignition time (
τ
Ign
), quasi steady combustion rate (
K
QS
) and global combustion rate (
K
Global
). The pre-ignition time was normalized with the square of the initial droplet diameter to offset any size effect between experimental repetitions. The pre-ignition time is dictated by the nature of fuel volatility, which depends on the boiling point and enthalpy of vaporization. As Jet-A contained components with higher volatility than Dodecane, it experienced earlier ignition. On the other hand, Dodecane had higher
K
QS
and
K
Global
compared to Jet-A. The combustion rate is primarily influenced by how easily a fuel can vaporize and be consumed in the flame region. This, in turn, depends on key factors such as the enthalpy of vaporization, which determines the energy required for the fuel to transition from liquid to vapor; the net heat of combustion, which indicates the total energy released during combustion; and the liquid phase density, which affects the ease with which the fuel vaporizes. Since the gas-phase properties of hydrocarbon fuels do not vary significantly, these liquid-phase properties play a critical role in determining the combustion rate. According to the classical
d
2
-law of combustion,
K
QS
is proportional to fuel density (
ρ l
), gas-phase specific heat capacity (
C
p , g
) and gas-phase thermal conductivity (
k g
) and can be expressed as
K
QS
∼
k
g
ρ
l
C
p , g
which can be further simplified as
K
QS
∼
1
ρ
l
, signifying that fuel with higher density should have lower combustion rate [67]. The relative liquid-phase density ratio (φρl) between two fuels should match the
K
QS
ratio (φKQS). The liquid-phase densities of Dodecane and Jet-A were 750 and 800 kg/m3, respectively, while the
K
QS
were 0.796 and 0.761 mm2/s, respectively. The calculated φKQS is 1.046, which is close to φρl=1.06.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combustion dynamics of base fuel and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: Since a surfactant (Span80) was added to prepare stable nanofuel, it is crucial to investigate its effects on the combustion dynamics of base fuel droplets to isolate its impact from that of G-CDs. The concentration-dependent effect of Span80 on the combustion behavior of liquid fuel droplets, particularly regarding burning rate, pre-ignition time, and puffing initiation, has been previously reported [11–13]. This section reports the surfactant's influence on both single-component and multi-component base fuels. Three surfactant concentrations were tested: 0.05%, 0.50%, and 2.50% w/w. The 0.50% w/w concentration represented the highest level used in nanofuel preparation, while the 2.50% w/w concentration helped to determine if the surfactant concentration had any noticeable effects on the combustion dynamics of the base fuels.

Fig. 6
a shows the temporal evolution of a Dodecane-Span80 droplet with 0.50% w/w surfactant concentration ("DS0.50"). Fig. 6 b shows the temporal changes of a Jet A-Span80 droplet under the same conditions ("JS0.50"). The DS0.50 droplet exhibited smooth regression throughout the combustion process, maintaining a nearly spherical shape similar to pure Dodecane droplets. However, ignition occurred slightly earlier at
t /
d
0
2
=0.559 s/mm2. JS0.50 droplet behaved similarly to neat Jet-A droplet during steady combustion but showed distinct behavior during the unsteady stage. During this phase, the JS0.50 droplet experienced the formation and growth of larger bubbles, which led to significant droplet expansion. The (d/d 0)2 value more than doubled compared to neat Jet-A droplets, and the formation of ligament-like structure was observed at
t /
d
0
2
=1.960 s/mm2. Importantly, this strong puffing stage began earlier in the JS0.50 droplet than in the neat Jet-A droplet. Given the higher boiling point of Span80 (∼800 K) compared to Jet-A (∼425–555 K), during the strong puffing stage, the less volatile surfactant dictated the droplet surface temperature. This promoted intense superheating of the high volatile components of Jet-A near the droplet core, resulting in larger bubble growth and more pronounced breakup, including the formation of ligament structure. However, puffing occurred after the region of interest (ROI) for quantifying droplet combustion parameters, indicating that it did not impact the quantitative measurements. In addition, the heat conduction from the support fiber could contribute significantly due to the low droplet-diameter-to-support-fiber-size ratio (
d /
d f
). For the DS0.50 droplet, the difference in boiling points between Dodecane (∼490 K) and Span80 was insufficient to induce superheating. Additionally, the surfactant concentration at 0.50% w/w was not high enough to trigger bubble formation, thus no puffing was observed. Fig. 6 c and 6d illustrate the corresponding
d
2
-plots of DS0.50 and JS0.50 droplets, demonstrating that the 0.50% w/w surfactant concentration did not noticeably impact the droplet dynamics of the base fuel throughout the ROI period.

Fig. 7
a illustrates the temporal changes of a Dodecane-Span80 droplet with a 2.50% w/w surfactant concentration ("DS2.50"). Fig. 7 b shows the temporal droplet morphology of a Jet A-Span80 droplet under the same conditions ("JS2.50"). The DS2.50 droplet exhibited smooth regression throughout the ROI period, maintaining a nearly spherical shape similar to neat Dodecane droplet. However, ignition again occurred earlier at
t /
d
0
2
=0.549 s/mm2. Most notably, DS2.50 experienced unsteady behavior starting from
t /
d
0
2
=1.837 s/mm2 with the onset of bubble formation. This bubble underwent a growth phase, expanding the droplet to almost double its initial size before puffing, eventually ejecting secondary droplets. Beyond
t /
d
0
2
=1.850 s/mm2, the puffing became intense with larger bubble formation. At
t /
d
0
2
=1.876 s/mm2, intense shape deformation was observed (
O
=0.243). At a 2.50% surfactant concentration, even a single-component base fuel droplet exhibited puffing behavior. The high surfactant concentration near the droplet surface elevated the surface temperature enough to trigger nucleation and bubble formation. However, this puffing occurred after the region of interest (ROI) for quantifying droplet combustion parameters, meaning it did not affect the quantitative measurements. Additionally, heat conduction from the support fiber likely had a significant influence due to the small droplet-to-fiber size ratio (
d /
d f
). Fig. 7 c compares the
d
2
-plot of DS2.50 droplets with that of neat Dodecane droplets, indicating that despite the unsteady stage, the droplet dynamics were largely similar to neat Dodecane throughout the ROI period.

In contrast, JS2.50 droplet exhibited puffing occurrences at an earlier stage around
t /
d
0
2
=1.167 s/mm2. The sufficient boiling point difference between Jet-A and Span80, along with the relatively high concentration of Span80, caused superheating of the more volatile components near the droplet core, accelerating the onset of puffing behavior. Intense shape deformation was noticeable earlier (at
t /
d
0
2
=1.314 s/mm2), followed by droplet oscillations occurring after secondary droplet ejection, which took place around
t /
d
0
2
=1.318 s/mm2. This puffing occurrence repeated in several cycles. In addition, multiple larger bubbles were observed stretching the droplet in various directions (at
t /
d
0
2
=1.591–1.597 s/mm2). Notably, after these vigorous cycles of strong puffing, a weaker puffing stage appeared near the end of combustion, forming multiple secondary droplets at
t /
d
0
2
=1.791 s/mm2. JS2.50 droplet also experienced earlier flame extinction due to significant fuel mass transportation to the flame zone in the form of secondary droplets, thereby decreasing the droplet lifetime. Fig. 6 d compares the
d
2
-plot of JS2.50 with that of neat Jet-A droplets, highlighting higher fluctuations in (d/d 0)2 during puffing compared to the other fuels discussed previously.

In Fig. 8
a, the average trends from five repetitions of Dodecane-Span80 droplets at three concentrations (0.05%, 0.50%, 2.50% w/w) are compared with neat Dodecane. Similarly, Fig. 8 b illustrates the average trends of five repetitions of Jet A-Span80 droplets at these three concentrations compared with neat Jet-A. DS0.05, DS0.50, DS2.50, JS0.05, JS0.50 droplets follow the
d
2
-law of combustion while JS2.50 droplet deviate from the
d
2
-law due to puffing. It is evident that even at high surfactant concentrations up to 2.50% w/w, there was no significant effect of surfactant on the droplet dynamics of single-component fuel. This consistency was observed in quantitative combustion parameters depicted in Fig. 8 c, showing normalized pre-ignition times, and Fig. 8 e, comparing quasi-steady and global combustion rates. For the studied surfactant concentrations with single-component base fuel, the quantified parameters fall within 5.00% of those observed for the base fuel.

When using a multi-component base fuel with high surfactant concentration (2.50% w/w), notable changes in combustion behavior were observed. Fig. 8 d highlights the effect of surfactant concentration on normalized pre-ignition times, while Fig. 8 f compares the quasi-steady and global combustion rates of Jet-A-Span80 mixtures. For surfactant concentrations of 0.05% and 0.50% w/w, there were no notable changes (<5.00%) in combustion rates and pre-ignition times compared to neat Jet-A. At 2.50% w/w surfactant concentration, there was a slight reduction in the quasi-steady combustion rate (∼5.56%), but the global combustion rate increased by 15.03% compared to neat Jet-A. The reduction in quasi-steady combustion rate can be explained by the behavior of surfactants at higher concentrations. Surfactants like Span80 tend to form layers on the droplet surface, which can act as a thermal barrier, slowing down evaporation. This occurs because Span80 has a higher heat of vaporization than the base fuel, which increases the energy required for evaporation and, in turn, reduces the overall evaporation rate [11]. Consequently, the slower evaporation reduces the subsequent combustion rate, as noted in previous studies [11,12].

In contrast to the quasi-steady combustion rate, the increased global combustion rate at higher surfactant concentrations (2.50% w/w) was primarily driven by more efficient fuel mass transport to the flame zone, facilitated by the ejection of secondary droplets during vigorous puffing. The onset of puffing behavior, marked by fluctuations in normalized droplet diameter, became more pronounced as surfactant concentrations increased, as observed in the JS0.05, JS0.50, and JS2.50 cases. Quantitatively, the puffing intensity for JS2.50 was measured at 8.30±1.53, with 28±2 occurrences up to the ROI time period. The method for quantifying puffing intensity and occurrences is detailed in S1.4: Puffing Intensity Quantification of the supplementary material [74,75]. This increase in puffing intensity and frequency, particularly at Span80 concentrations exceeding 1.00% w/w, is consistent with previous findings in alcohol-based nanofuels [11]. In contrast, no significant puffing events were observed for JS0.05 and JS0.50 droplets during the same time period.

At surfactant concentrations below 0.50% w/w, the impact on combustion dynamics for both single-component and multi-component fuels was minimal. However, at higher concentrations (2.50% w/w), combustion behavior significantly changed due to intense puffing. This phenomenon requires careful consideration when formulating nanofuels, as surfactant-induced puffing has been identified in some studies as a mechanism that enhances nanoparticle combustion in the flame zone [3,11,12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combustion dynamics of G-CDs based nanofuels
Effect of G-CDs mass concentration on single component base fuel
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of G-CDs on the droplet combustion dynamics of Dodecane was investigated across seven different mass concentrations, ranging from 0.01% to 1.00% w/w. Fig. 9
a and 9b show the temporal changes in droplet morphology for DCD0.075 and DCD0.50 droplets, respectively. DCD0.075 droplet exhibited notably earlier ignition (
t /
d
0
2
=0.537 s/mm2) compared to neat Dodecane droplet (
t /
d
0
2
=0.569 s/mm2), while DCD0.50 droplet ignited slightly later (
t /
d
0
2
=0.588 s/mm2). Both DCD0.075 and DCD0.50 droplets regressed linearly in size over time following the
d
2
-law of combustion, maintaining almost spherical shape (
O
>0.950) before flame extinction. No bubble formation or puffing was observed for the tested concentrations. Two main mechanisms can lead to bubble formation in nanofuels prepared from single-component base fuel: (1) Dispersed NPs or NP aggregates can act as localized heterogeneous nucleation sites and initiate bubble formation, and (2) accumulation of NPs near the droplet surface, forming an impermeable crust that raises the droplet surface temperature. This can lead to the superheating of fuel components near the droplet core, while the crust allows the droplet to withstand internal pressure buildup. However, based on the observed droplet morphology of DCD0.075 and DCD0.50, neither superheating nor crust formation was evident in these cases. Fig. 9 c compares the
d
2
-plots of DCD0.075 and DCD0.50 with neat Dodecane, showing that both nanofuel droplets regressed linearly over time, similar to neat Dodecane, but with differing regression rates. DCD0.075 regressed faster than both DCD0.50 and neat Dodecane, while DCD0.50 regressed slightly faster than neat Dodecane.

Fig. 9 d presents the averaged trend of five repetitions of
d
2
-plots of all Dodecane-GCDs nanofuels along with neat Dodecane. All nanofuels adhere to the
d
2
-law of combustion. Fig. 9 e compares the pre-ignition times of nanofuels with neat Dodecane, showing a nonlinear dependence of pre-ignition time on particle concentration. This suggests that particle concentration influenced pre-ignition time in a complex manner, with certain concentrations having a more significant impact on the combustion process. Pre-ignition time, as defined in S1.3: Pre-Ignition Time and Combustion Period Quantification of the supplementary material, refers to the time duration from ignition mechanism initiation to the appearance of a stable flame. It can be divided into two components: the physical delay and the chemical delay. The physical delay includes processes such as droplet heating, evaporation, and fuel vapor-air mixing, while the chemical delay is linked to pre-combustion reactions within the fuel–air mixture. At concentrations of 0.10% w/w or less, pre-ignition times decreased by 7.00 to 10.00%, primarily due to enhanced radiation absorption and improved heat transfer both at the surface and within the droplet. This facilitated faster heating and evaporation, leading to shorter pre-ignition time [5,11,12,16,19,21,68]. As no puffing or secondary droplet ejection was observed during this period, it indicates that the nanoparticles stayed within the droplet and did not affect pre-combustion reactions or air–fuel mixing. Therefore, the reduction in pre-ignition delay can be primarily attributed to changes in the physical delay, particularly the increased fuel evaporation before ignition. However, when the nanoparticle concentration ranged from 0.10% to 0.50% w/w, an increase in pre-ignition times was observed, beginning at 0.25% w/w. This was due to the higher particle density near the droplet surface, which reduced the effective surface area available for evaporation [5,19]. As a result, evaporation slowed, leading to longer pre-ignition times. At higher concentrations, particularly at 1.00% w/w, particle aggregation near the droplet surface became more pronounced, further reducing the evaporation rate. At this concentration, the pre-ignition time increased by approximately 9.63%. This non-linear, concentration-dependent trend in pre-ignition times has been previously observed with various nanoparticles [2,3,57,58,68–70]. In the case of G-CDs, a notable reduction in pre-ignition time was observed at much lower concentrations compared to other nanoparticles (as compared in Fig. 15 b).

Fig. 9 f compares the
K
QS
, and Fig. 9 g compares the
K
Global
of nanofuels with neat Dodecane. The
K
QS
followed a non-linear trend with increasing particle concentration, initially increasing up to 0.50% w/w and then decreasing at 1.00% w/w. At concentrations of 0.10% w/w or lower,
K
QS
increased by 4.00% to 16.00%, driven by enhanced heat absorption from the flame and improved heat transfer within the droplet. At these concentrations, the low particle number density near the droplet surface prevented significant nanoparticle aggregation and accumulation, facilitating more efficient fuel evaporation [5,16,19,21]. The greatest improvement in
K
QS
was observed at 0.10% w/w G-CDs, reaching a maximum of 16.46%. As the concentration increased from 0.10% to 0.50% w/w, although the combustion rate decreased relative to 0.10% w/w, it remained higher than that of the base fuel. In this range, nanoparticle aggregation and accumulation near the droplet surface became more pronounced due to the higher particle number density [5]. This reduced the effective surface area available for evaporation, offsetting some of the improved thermal transport benefits. At concentrations above 0.50% w/w, the aggregation of particles near the droplet surface became significant, inhibiting fuel vaporization and resulting in a lower combustion rate compared to the base fuel. At a concentration of 1.00% w/w G-CDs, the
K
QS
decreased by approximately 5.63%. Similarly, the
K
Global
improved by about 19.14% at a 0.10% w/w G-CDs concentration but decreased by 8.21% at a 1.00% w/w G-CDs concentration. No puffing was observed in the single-component base fuel-based nanofuels, indicating that particles did not transport between the droplet and flame zone, and thus no significant catalytic or reactive effects were expected. The observed combustion trends can be attributed to the balance between enhanced heat absorption, improved heat transfer, and particle aggregation dynamics.

To analyze the relationship between NPs aggregation and combustion characteristics, the dimensionless number
C R
can be utilized, defined as the ratio of particle migration time (
τ p
) to droplet combustion time (
τ c
) [13,19]:
(2)
C R
=
τ p
τ c
(3)
τ p
=
L
m
2
2 D
p
where,
L m
=the mean distance between two adjacent particles=
(
V d
n
)
1 / 3
,
V d
=the volume of nanofuel droplet,
n
=number of NPs within the droplet,
D p
=diffusion coefficient of NPs=
k B
T
6 π μ r
,
k B
=Boltzmann’s Constant,
T
=droplet temperature, and
r
=radius of NPs.

First, for the same type of nanoparticles (NPs) with different sizes, using the same base fuel, similar droplet sizes, and the same particle concentration, the particles' migration time (
τ p
​) follows a proportional relation to the cube of the NPs' size. This indicates that particle size significantly impacts migration dynamics, with larger particles taking considerably longer to migrate within the droplet compared to smaller ones. On the other hand, for the same type of NPs with the same sizes, same base fuel, similar droplet sizes, and different particle concentrations, the particles' migration time (
τ p
​) is inversely proportional to the two-thirds power of the particle concentration. This means that as particle concentration increases, the migration time decreases accordingly. For G-CDs, characterized by their very small size (∼3.00 nm), the calculated particle migration times were significantly shorter than the overall combustion period:
τ p
≈64.00 μs (0.01% w/w),
τ p
≈13.00 μs (0.10% w/w),
τ p
≈9.00 μs (0.50% w/w),
τ p
≈6.00 μs (1.00% w/w), compared to other reported values with larger NPs [2–4,13]. The notably small migration times for these particles enabled rapid redistribution within the droplet, facilitating the formation of a uniform network of aggregates. Consequently, at lower G-CDs concentrations (≤0.50% w/w), the aggregation was relatively minimal, leading to enhanced combustion rates with increasing particle concentration. However, as the particle concentration increased, the particle migration time continued to decrease. This reduction implied a decrease in the inter-particle distance, which resulted in a higher collision frequency and consequently more intense aggregation. The observed decline in combustion rate with higher particle concentrations indicated that the formed aggregates had reached a size that hinders the fuel vaporization process. Although the particle migration times remain similar in order of magnitude for concentrations between 0.10% and 1.00% w/w, the combustion rate started to decrease noticeably from 0.25% w/w.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of G-CDs mass concentration on multi-component base fuel
ce:section.ce:para:
Transitioning from analyzing single-component fuels to more complex fuels like Jet-A enables a more comprehensive examination of how CDs (G-CDs) affect the combustion dynamics of liquid fuels. Fig. 10
a and 10b represent the temporal changes in droplet morphology for JCD0.10 and JCD1.00 droplets, respectively. The JCD0.10 droplet exhibited earlier ignition (
t /
d
0
2
=0.505 s/mm2) compared to the neat Jet-A droplet (
t /
d
0
2
=0.530 s/mm2), while the JCD1.00 droplet ignited later (
t /
d
0
2
=0.586 s/mm2). After ignition, both JCD0.10 and JCD1.00 droplets regressed smoothly during the steady combustion stage, maintaining a nearly spherical shape (
O
>0.988). However, the onset of nucleation and bubble formation appeared much earlier compared to neat Jet-A and Jet-A/Span80 droplets (JS0.05 and JS0.50). For the JCD0.10 droplet, the first bubble nucleation occurred near the droplet surface at
t /
d
0
2
=1.226 s/mm2 and was quickly transported to the droplet surface, resulting in weak puffing at
t /
d
0
2
=1.230 s/mm2 without significant shape deformation. Later puffing occurrences were accompanied by shape deformation (
O
≈0.91) and periodic oscillations (
t /
d
0
2
=1.274–1.277 s/mm2). These oscillations resulted from the rapid release of internal vapor pressure, further affecting the droplet’s shape.

At around
t /
d
0
2
=1.463 s/mm2, a strong puffing stage emerged, characterized by significant shape deformation, periodic oscillations in multiple directions, the formation of a jellyfish-like structure, and the development of a ligament structure, which eventually led to the ejection of a secondary droplet (
t /
d
0
2
=1.464–1.646 s/mm2). Large bubbles, similar to those observed in the Jet-A/Span80 mixture, formed near the end of combustion, likely due to the increased concentration of low volatile surfactant. For the JCD1.00 droplet, bubble formation began even earlier (
t /
d
0
2
=1.102 s/mm2). This was followed by multiple bubble formations (
t /
d
0
2
=1.281 s/mm2), bubble coalescence (
t /
d
0
2
=1.282 s/mm2), and the breakup of the merged bubble (
t /
d
0
2
=1.302 s/mm2), forming secondary droplets and causing droplet oscillation (
t /
d
0
2
=1.427–1.428 s/mm2). This led to strong puffing events, characterized by higher shape deformation and multiple stretching, and squeezing of droplets (
t /
d
0
2
=1.696–1.934 s/mm2) before flame extinction.

Fig. 10 c presents the
d
2
-plot of neat Jet-A, JCD0.10, and JCD1.00 droplets, showing that the inclusion of G-CDs facilitated heterogeneous nucleation inside the droplet, particularly near the surface where most thermal radiation was absorbed. The onset of bubble formation due to heterogeneous nucleation occurred earlier with increased particle concentration, driven by the low particle migration time and the formation of aggregates that could initiate nucleation, as shown in Fig. 10 d. All the nanofuels exhibited unsteady combustion stages, including both weak and strong puffing phases. Fig. 11
a compares the pre-ignition time of Jet-A/GCDs nanofuels with neat Jet-A. Similar to single-component fuel-based nanofuels, a non-linear, particle-concentration-dependent trend was observed. The highest reduction in pre-ignition, 8.38%, was noted at 0.05% w/w. However, starting at a particle concentration of 0.25% w/w, the pre-ignition time began to increase due to particle or aggregate accumulation effects, with the highest increase, 9.62%, observed at 1.00% w/w.

Fig. 11 b shows the concentration-dependent trends in the
K
QS
during stage 2 combustion, while Fig. 11 c illustrates the trend in the
K
Global
. At concentrations of 0.10% w/w or less, there was a significant increase in combustion rate (ranging from 4.00% to 20.00%). In this range, the enhancement in thermal radiation absorption and heat transfer within the burning droplet outweighed the negative effects of particle aggregation, leading to an improved combustion rate. The highest increase in the
K
QS
was observed at 0.075% w/w, with a 19.76% increase. Between 0.10% and 0.50% w/w, the combustion rate reached a plateau, likely caused by the saturation of particles near the droplet surface, where accumulation and aggregation began to dominate. However, the combustion rate still exceeded that of the base fuel. Beyond 0.50% w/w, particle aggregation became more dominant, leading to a 5.38% reduction in
K
QS
at 1.00% w/w. This indicates that at concentrations beyond saturation, adding more particles causes a decline in
K
QS
due to the accumulation of particles and aggregates on the droplet surface, which hinders liquid fuel vaporization.

The overall effect of varying particle concentrations on the combustion rate for multi-component base fuels is represented by the global combustion rate constant (
K
Global
) as shown in Fig. 13 . A notable enhancement in the global combustion rate, ranging from 3.00% to 16.00%, was observed for particle concentrations up to 0.50% w/w. This improvement was due to the combined effects of enhanced thermal transport, puffing phenomena, and aggregation dynamics within the droplet. For concentrations greater than 0.10% w/w, the contribution of puffing becomes more apparent, as indicated by significantly higher global puffing intensity and increased puffing occurrences compared to lower concentrations (Fig. 12).

Fig. 14 illustrates the changes in combustion rates (both quasi-steady and global) compared to Jet-A across different stages. Notably, the most substantial enhancements were observed for JCD0.075 and JCD0.10 across all stages, highlighting their potential for improving fuel combustion performance.

Fig. 15 presents a comparison of the percentage changes in combustion rate and pre-ignition time from the current study with values reported in the literature for carbon nanoparticles and various base fuels [19–21,57,58,69–73]. Given the differences in experimental methods, preparation techniques, and suspension methods, which can significantly impact combustion rates and pre-ignition times, this comparison is based on percentage changes relative to the base fuel for consistency. The trends observed in combustion rates and pre-ignition times in this study align generally with previously reported findings. However, the maximum enhancement in combustion rate observed here is higher than in most other studies, likely due to the smaller size of G-CDs compared to the nanoparticles used in other research. A comprehensive list of existing literature on carbon nanoparticles and their effects on liquid fuel droplet combustion behavior, along with relevant information, is also provided in Table S2 .

G-CDs impact combustion dynamics differently in single-component and multi-component fuels, with their effects dependent on concentration. In single-component fuels, G-CDs improve radiation absorption and enhance heat transfer within burning droplet, resulting in higher combustion rates and shorter pre-ignition times at lower concentrations (≤0.10% w/w). However, at higher concentrations, particle accumulation and aggregation near the droplet surface creates thermal barriers that impede vaporization, leading to reduced combustion efficiency, lower combustion rates, and longer pre-ignition times. In multi-component fuels, G-CDs not only improve radiation absorption and heat transfer but also induce complex phenomena such as puffing acting as heterogeneous nucleation sites. At low concentrations, these effects enhance combustion rates, but at higher concentrations, G-CDs aggregation near the droplet surface, along with the fuel’s complex vaporization dynamics, leads to more intense puffing and secondary droplet ejections, which can affect combustion efficiency.

Gel-like carbon dots (G-CDs) have been investigated for their impact on droplet combustion behaviors in liquid fuels, with Dodecane and Jet-A representing single-component and multi-component fuels, respectively. The study also explored the influence of surfactants on combustion characteristics. Key findings include:
•
Low concentrations of surfactants (up to 0.50% w/w) acted as stabilizers without affecting combustion behavior. However, at a 2.50% w/w concentration, multi-component fuels exhibited intense puffing, with the global combustion rate increasing by approximately 15.00% due to higher puffing intensity and frequency.
•
G-CDs enhanced combustion dynamics of single-component fuels at concentrations up to 0.10% w/w. Combustion rates increased by 4.00% to 16.00%, and pre-ignition times decreased by 7.00% to 10.00%, primarily due to improved heat absorption and transfer. The highest combustion rate increase (16.46%) occurred at 0.10% w/w, while the greatest reduction in pre-ignition time (10.19%) was observed at 0.075% w/w. However, for concentrations between 0.10% and 0.50% w/w, although combustion rates remained higher than the base fuel, they declined as particle concentration increased due to nanoparticle accumulation and aggregation. Above 0.50% w/w, where particle accumulation near the droplet surface intensified, combustion rates further decreased, showing a 5.63% reduction at 1.00% w/w. No puffing or micro-explosions were observed, indicating no significant catalytic effects; the trends primarily reflect the balance between better heat absorption and heat transfer and less intense nanoparticle aggregation.
•
G-CDs also enhanced the combustion dynamics of multi-component fuels, though with more complexity due to the onset of strong and weak puffing stages.
–
In the steady stage, a non-linear, concentration-dependent trend in combustion rates and pre-ignition times was observed. For concentrations less or equal to 0.10% w/w, combustion rates increased by 4.00% to 20.00%, with pre-ignition times reduced by 3.00% to 8.00%. The highest combustion rate increase (19.76%) occurred at 0.075% w/w, while the greatest pre-ignition time reduction (8.38%) was at 0.05% w/w. Between 0.10% and 0.50% w/w, a plateau was reached as particle aggregation effects increased. Beyond 0.50% w/w, particle aggregation dominated, reducing the combustion rate by 5.38% at 1.00% w/w and increasing pre-ignition time by 9.62%.
–
In the weak puffing stage, the highest combustion rate enhancement was observed at 0.10% w/w (20.45%). Puffing events facilitated additional fuel mass transport through secondary droplets, alongside the effects of heat absorption and transfer. No plateau in combustion rates was seen up to 0.50% w/w, but increased aggregation resulted in a 7.74% reduction at 1.00% w/w.
–
In the strong puffing stage, strong puffing events led to significantly higher puffing intensity and frequency compared to the weak puffing stage. Combustion rates increased by 6.00% to 27.00% for concentrations between 0.01% and 0.50% w/w. Beyond 0.50% w/w, combustion rates remained higher than the base fuel.
•
The influence of G-CDs on multi-component fuel combustion was distinct from their effects on single-component fuels. In single-component fuels, heat absorption, heat transfer, and nanoparticle aggregation were the primary factors affecting combustion. However, in multi-component fuels, additional complexities, such as the initiation and frequent occurrences of puffing, played a significant role. These puffing events, with increased intensity and frequency as particle concentration rose, had a notable impact on the overall combustion behavior.

This study provides insights into the potential of G-CDs to impact combustion behaviors of liquid fuels and offers essential experimental data for further understanding their applications in fuel combustion processes.
